quiz_question: In what year did the phrase 'information technology' first appear in its modern sense?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term 'information technology' in its modern sense first appeared in a 1958 article published in the Harvard Business Review by Harold J. Leavitt and Thomas L. Whisler. Personal computers wouldn't arrive until the late 1970s, making this a prescient coinage.

quiz_question: What two-letter abbreviation stands for 'Information Technology'?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the short name for information technology is 'IT'. Despite being a modern coinage from a 1958 article, IT now encompasses an enormous global industry including software, hardware, networks, data centers, and support services.

quiz_question: Who first coined the term 'biotechnology' in 1919?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term biotechnology was first used by Károly Ereky in 1919 to refer to the production of products from raw materials with the aid of living organisms. DNA's double helix wasn't described until 1953 — meaning 'biotechnology' as a term predated the science's central molecule by 34 years.

quiz_question: Which ancient process makes beer, wine, and cheese forms of biotechnology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fermentation is a key biotechnology technique used to produce products like beer, wine, and cheese. This means humans have been practicing biotechnology for thousands of years — long before the term was coined in 1919.

quiz_question: What biotechnology technique allows genes from one organism to be inserted into another?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, one of biotechnology's key techniques is genetic engineering, which allows scientists to modify the genetic makeup of organisms to achieve desired outcomes — including inserting genes from one organism into another to create new traits or modify existing ones.

quiz_question: How many companies have historically manufactured hard disk drives?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, more than 224 companies have produced HDDs historically, though after extensive industry consolidation, most units are now manufactured by just Seagate, Toshiba, and Western Digital. This is one of the most dramatic market consolidations in tech history.

quiz_question: What type of storage do smartphones and tablets use instead of hard disk drives?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, HDDs maintained their dominant position in servers and PCs, but personal computing devices produced in large volume — like mobile phones and tablets — rely on flash memory storage devices. Flash has no moving parts, is smaller, and uses less power.

quiz_question: What natural biological structures are also classified as machines?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term 'machine' is commonly applied to artificial devices but also encompasses natural biological macromolecules — known as molecular machines — that perform mechanical work inside living cells.

quiz_question: What is the defining effect that all machines produce?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the defining 'has effect' of a machine is energy transformation. This applies to everything from a simple lever to a car engine — each converts energy from one form to another.

quiz_question: How many monthly active users does Android have worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Android has the largest installed base of any operating system in the world with over three billion monthly active users, accounting for 46% of the global operating system market.

quiz_question: Who was Android OS partly named after?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Android was named after Andrew Rubin (and the word 'android' meaning robot). Rubin was one of Android's founders alongside Rich Miner and the Open Handset Alliance before Google acquired it.

quiz_question: In what year was Android first released to the public?
explanation: According to Wikidata (inception: 2008-09-23), Android was first released on September 23, 2008. Wikipedia confirms that since 2011 it has been the most used operating system on smartphones worldwide.

quiz_question: When did global nuclear stockpiles reach their all-time peak of over 64,000 weapons?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the total from all nuclear stockpiles peaked at over 64,000 weapons in 1986 and is now around 9,600 — a reduction of roughly 85% through arms control treaties and disarmament.

quiz_question: Who invented the first web browser in 1990?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the web browser was invented by Tim Berners-Lee with a time of discovery on 1990-12-20. He created both the browser and the World Wide Web simultaneously at CERN, then gave both away for free.

quiz_question: How many people globally had used a web browser as of 2023?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of 2023 an estimated 5.4 billion people had used a browser, representing the vast majority of the world's internet-connected population.

quiz_question: Who coined the word 'robot' in 1920?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the word 'robot' was named by Josef Čapek with the earliest written record in 1920. The word comes from the Czech 'robota' meaning drudgery or forced labor, and was used in his brother Karel's famous play R.U.R.

quiz_question: What was the name of the world's first commercial programmable robot?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first commercial, digital and programmable robot was built by George Devol in 1954 and named the Unimate. It was sold to General Motors in 1961, where it lifted hot metal from die casting machines.

quiz_question: In what year were the first electronic autonomous robots created?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first electronic autonomous robots were created by William Grey Walter in Bristol, England, in 1948 — predating the first commercial robot by six years.

quiz_question: In what year did Linus Torvalds begin developing the Linux kernel?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Linus Torvalds is a Finnish-American software engineer born December 28, 1969, who created the Linux kernel in 1991. Linux now powers most of the world's servers, supercomputers, and Android phones.

quiz_question: What version control system did Linux creator Linus Torvalds also invent?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Torvalds also created the distributed version control system Git. Today Git is used by virtually all software developers worldwide, and platforms like GitHub and GitLab are built on top of it.

quiz_question: Who was Linus Torvalds named after?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Linus Torvalds was named after Linus Pauling, the Nobel Prize-winning chemist who is the only person to have won two unshared Nobel Prizes (Chemistry in 1954 and Peace in 1962).

quiz_question: Who invented the computer mouse?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the computer mouse was invented by Douglas Engelbart (inception: 1963), with William English as a contributor. The first public demo came five years later in 1968 at the 'Mother of All Demos'.

quiz_question: How did the very first computer mice track movement before using a rolling ball?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, mice originally used two separate wheels to track movement — one in the x-dimension, one in the y. The standard design later shifted to a ball rolling on a surface, and then to the optical movement detection used in modern mice.

quiz_question: In what year was the transistor invented at Bell Labs?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the transistor's time of discovery was 1947-12-01. Physicists John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley at Bell Labs shared the 1956 Nobel Prize in Physics for its invention.

quiz_question: When was the field-effect transistor concept first proposed — 22 years before it could be built?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, physicist Julius Edgar Lilienfeld proposed the concept of a field-effect transistor (FET) in 1925, but it was not possible to construct a working device at that time. The first working transistor came in 1947.

quiz_question: What device did transistors replace, enabling the modern computing revolution?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, transistors revolutionized electronics by replacing vacuum tubes — following them in lineage according to Wikidata. Compared to vacuum tubes, transistors are generally smaller and require less power, paving the way for compact computers.

quiz_question: In what year was the term 'information technology' first used in print?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term 'information technology' in its modern sense first appeared in a 1958 article in the Harvard Business Review by Harold J. Leavitt and Thomas L. Whisler, who defined three categories: processing techniques, statistical methods, and computer simulation.

quiz_question: Besides computers and networks, which two older technologies are part of information technology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while IT is commonly associated with computers and networks, the term also encompasses other information distribution technologies such as television and telephones.

quiz_question: Who coined the word 'biotechnology' in 1919?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term biotechnology was first used by Károly Ereky in 1919 to refer to the production of products from raw materials with the aid of living organisms.

quiz_question: What ancient human practice is considered the earliest form of biotechnology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, biotechnology encompasses procedures going back to domestication of animals, cultivation of plants, and artificial selection and hybridization — practiced long before the term was coined in 1919.

quiz_question: What biotechnology process produces both beer and medical antibiotics?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fermentation is a key biotechnology technique used to produce a wide range of products such as beer, wine, and cheese, as well as medicines. The same basic biological process links ancient brewing to modern pharmaceutical manufacturing.

quiz_question: In what year did IBM introduce the world's first hard disk drive?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata (time of discovery: 1956), IBM introduced the first disk drive — the 350 for the 305 RAMAC — in 1956. Hard disks became the dominant secondary storage for general-purpose computers beginning in the early 1960s.

quiz_question: In what year did solid-state drive revenues first overtake hard disk drive revenues?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the revenues for SSDs (most using NAND flash memory) slightly exceeded those for HDDs in 2018. SSDs offer higher data-transfer rates, better reliability, lower latency, and smaller size than traditional hard drives.

quiz_question: How many companies historically produced hard disk drives?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, more than 224 companies have produced HDDs historically, though after extensive industry consolidation most units are now manufactured by Seagate, Toshiba, and Western Digital.

quiz_question: How many simple machines did Renaissance philosophers identify?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Renaissance natural philosophers identified six simple machines as the elementary devices that put a load into motion and calculated mechanical advantage. These are the lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, and screw.

quiz_question: What natural biological structures are classified as machines?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term 'machine' is commonly applied to both artificial devices and natural biological macromolecules, such as molecular machines found inside living cells. These nanoscale biological machines perform tasks like copying DNA and generating cellular energy.

quiz_question: How many separate device categories has the smartphone largely replaced?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, smartphones have largely replaced PDAs, handheld PCs, portable media players, point-and-shoot cameras, camcorders, handheld game consoles, e-readers, pocket calculators, and GPS units — shrinking the consumer electronics industry's most popular categories into one device.

quiz_question: What share of the world population used smartphones as of 2020?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of 2020, 75.05 percent of the world population were smartphone users. In 2019 alone, 1.54 billion smartphone units were shipped worldwide, with over a billion sold globally every year since 2014.

quiz_question: What design shift did the iPhone trigger in the smartphone industry?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, following the rising popularity of the iPhone in the late 2000s, the majority of smartphones adopted thin, slate-like form factors with large capacitive touchscreens and multi-touch gesture support, rather than physical keyboards. This fundamentally reshaped the entire industry.

quiz_question: At what size range does nanotechnology operate, in nanometers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nanotechnology involves manipulating matter with at least one dimension sized from 1 to 100 nanometers. At this nanoscale, surface area and quantum mechanical effects become the dominant factors in describing material properties.

quiz_question: Which medical application field uses nanotechnology to treat disease?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nanotechnology may create new materials and devices with applications in nanomedicine, nanoelectronics, agricultural sectors, biomaterials energy production, and consumer products. The field also raises concerns about nanomaterial toxicity and environmental impact.

quiz_question: What unit of length gives nanotechnology its name?
explanation: According to Wikidata, nanotechnology is named after the nanometre (nm), which is one billionth of a metre. The 'nano' prefix means one billionth, so nanotechnology literally means 'technology at the billionth-of-a-metre scale'.

quiz_question: Since what century have condoms been used to prevent disease transmission?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, condoms as a method of preventing STIs have been used since at least 1564. Rubber condoms became available in 1855, followed by latex condoms in the 1920s — making them one of history's longest-standing public health tools.

quiz_question: Which contraceptive device appears on the WHO's List of Essential Medicines?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the condom is on the World Health Organization's List of Essential Medicines. As of 2019, globally around 21% of those using birth control use the condom, making it the second-most common method after female sterilization.

quiz_question: What is the annual pregnancy rate with perfect condom use?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, with proper use at every act of intercourse, women whose partners use external condoms experience a 2% per-year pregnancy rate. With typical use, the rate rises to 18% per year — highlighting the gap between perfect and real-world use.

quiz_question: What obsolete technology did computer keyboards replace for inputting data?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, keyboard-based interaction replaced early punched cards and paper tape technology, becoming the main computer input method since the 1970s. The mouse supplemented keyboards from the 1980s, and touchscreens arrived in the 2000s.

quiz_question: What does a keyboard actually send to a computer when a key is pressed?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in a modern computer the interpretation of key presses is left to the software. The keyboard sends only a scan code — information about which physical key was pressed or released — and the software decides what character that corresponds to. This is why the same key can type different characters in different layouts.

quiz_question: Why is physical computer equipment called 'hardware'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, hardware derived its name from the fact that it is hard or rigid with respect to changes, whereas software is soft because it is easy to change. This naming elegantly captures the fundamental difference between physical components and written instructions.

quiz_question: What software is permanently embedded inside computer hardware components?
explanation: According to Wikidata, hardware has firmware as a part — a type of software permanently stored in the physical device. Firmware bridges the gap between pure hardware and software: it is changeable like software, but stored on the hardware itself, earning the name 'firm' (neither fully hard nor fully soft).

quiz_question: In what year was the first computer network created?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first computer network was created in 1940 when George Stibitz connected a terminal at Dartmouth to his Complex Number Calculator at Bell Labs in New York — predating the internet by decades.

quiz_question: What engineering trade-off defines how a tractor delivers its power?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a tractor is an engineering vehicle specifically designed to deliver a high tractive effort at slow speeds for hauling trailers or machinery. This is the opposite of most vehicles, which prioritize speed over pulling force.

quiz_question: What was the original primary purpose of the farm tractor?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the tractor most commonly describes a farm vehicle that provides power and traction to mechanize agricultural tasks, especially and originally tillage. Agricultural implements may be towed behind or mounted on the tractor for many other purposes today.

quiz_question: What did Amazon originally sell when Jeff Bezos founded it in 1994?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Amazon was founded in 1994 by Jeff Bezos in Bellevue, Washington, and originally started as an online marketplace for books before gradually expanding its offerings to include a wide range of product categories, earning the nickname 'The Everything Store'.

quiz_question: Which company did Amazon overtake in 2021 to become the world's largest retailer?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2021 Amazon surpassed Walmart as the world's largest retailer outside of China, driven in large part by its paid subscription plan, Amazon Prime, which has 200 million subscribers worldwide.

quiz_question: How much did Amazon pay to acquire Whole Foods in 2017?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Amazon's acquisition of Whole Foods in August 2017 for US$13.4 billion substantially increased its market share and presence as a physical retailer — a striking move for a company that built its empire on avoiding physical stores.

quiz_question: What made Unix the first portable operating system in 1973?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Unix distinguished itself from predecessors as the first portable operating system because almost the entire OS was written in the C programming language in 1973, allowing Unix to operate on numerous hardware platforms.

quiz_question: Where was Unix originally developed in 1969?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Unix development started in 1969 at the Bell Labs research center, primarily by Ken Thompson, Dennis Ritchie, and others. Initially intended for use inside the Bell System, AT&T later licensed Unix to outside parties in the late 1970s, spawning dozens of variants.

quiz_question: Which widely used operating system descends directly from Unix?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Linux is listed as a derivative work of Unix. The Unix philosophy of modular, simple tools directly influenced Linux, which today powers most web servers, supercomputers, and the Android operating system used on the majority of smartphones globally.

quiz_question: What conflict arose in the computing world from Unix's many competing versions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, amid the 'Unix wars' of standardization, AT&T alongside Sun merged System V, BSD, SunOS, and Xenix into UNIX System V Release 4 (SVR4) in 1989 to consolidate the fragmented ecosystem. The conflict arose because multiple vendors each developed their own Unix variants with incompatible features.

quiz_question: What system translates human-readable hostnames into numeric network addresses?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, hosts may have hostnames — memorable labels — which can be mapped to a network address using a hosts file or a name server such as Domain Name Service (DNS). DNS is essentially the phone book of the internet.

quiz_question: Besides the global internet, what type of network connects computers inside everyday devices?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, today almost all computers are connected to a computer network such as the global Internet or embedded networks found in modern electronic devices. Many applications have only limited functionality unless connected to a network.

quiz_question: What Japanese company's order prompted Intel to create the first microprocessor?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, pocket-sized calculators became available in the 1970s especially after the Intel 4004 — the first microprocessor — was developed by Intel for the Japanese calculator company Busicom. A desk-calculator commission accidentally birthed the modern computing era.

quiz_question: In 1986, what percentage of global computing capacity did calculators represent?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1986 calculators still represented an estimated 41% of the world's general-purpose hardware capacity to compute information. By 2007, that figure had diminished to less than 0.05% — a collapse driven by smartphones and PCs.

quiz_question: What did the first solid-state electronic calculator replace as the primary arithmetic tool?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first solid-state electronic calculator was created in the early 1960s. Wikidata confirms the start time as 1960 and notes it replaces the adding machine. Previously, arithmetic was handled by mechanical adding machines — solid-state technology made computation silent, compact, and far faster.

quiz_question: What CPU component actually performs all arithmetic and logic operations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the arithmetic–logic unit (ALU) performs arithmetic and logic operations within the CPU. The control unit orchestrates the process, but the ALU is the engine that actually crunches every number and evaluates every condition in every program ever run.

quiz_question: What term describes a CPU chip containing multiple processor cores?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, microprocessor chips with multiple CPUs are called multi-core processors (MCPs). Individual physical CPUs within the chip are called processor cores and can also be multithreaded for further parallelism.

quiz_question: What purpose do large on-chip caches serve in a modern CPU?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, modern CPUs devote a lot of semiconductor area to caches and instruction-level parallelism to increase performance. Without caches, a fast CPU would spend most of its time stalled waiting for data from comparatively slow main memory.

quiz_question: What is the software term for investigating and fixing problems in code?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, debugging means investigating and fixing problems in code, listed as an auxiliary task of programming distinct from writing code itself. The term dates from early computing and one of the earliest known 'bugs' was a literal moth found inside a 1947 Navy computer.

quiz_question: What is the term for a step-by-step procedure specification in programming?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, programming involves designing and implementing algorithms, described as step-by-step specifications of procedures. Wikidata notes that programming follows pseudocode, which is an informal algorithm notation. The word 'algorithm' is over 1,200 years old, derived from 9th-century mathematician Al-Khwarizmi's name.

quiz_question: What term applies when software development uses formal engineering design processes?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sometimes software development is known as software engineering, especially when it employs formal methods or follows an engineering design process. The distinction matters in regulated industries like aerospace or medical devices where lives depend on correctness.

quiz_question: What Italian word meaning 'bobbin' gave rockets their name?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'rocket' derives from Italian rocchetto, literally meaning 'bobbin/spool', named for its shape. The cylindrical form of early firework rockets matched the wooden spools used in weaving — an unlikely origin for humanity's most powerful vehicles.

quiz_question: Why can rockets operate in space when jet engines cannot?
correct_answer: Because they carry their own oxidizer
explanation: According to Wikipedia, rockets are fuelled entirely by propellant which they carry, without the need for oxygen from air. Rocket engines actually operate more efficiently outside the atmosphere because there is no air drag to fight.

quiz_question: Where and when did military rocket use first originate?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, rockets for military and recreational uses date back to at least 13th-century China. Significant scientific and industrial use only came in the 20th century, when rocketry enabled the Space Age — nearly 700 years later.

quiz_question: Who designed the first modern internal combustion engine in 1876?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first modern internal combustion engine — the Otto engine — was designed in 1876 by German engineer Nicolaus Otto. The four-stroke cycle he developed is still the basis of almost every car engine today, 150 years later.

quiz_question: What unusual fuel did Rudolf Diesel use in his engines as early as 1900?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as early as 1900 the inventor of the diesel engine, Rudolf Diesel, was using peanut oil to run his engines. This makes Diesel one of the earliest advocates of biofuel — over 120 years before the concept became a 21st-century talking point.

quiz_question: What combustion pattern distinguishes jet engines from typical car piston engines?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term 'internal combustion engine' usually refers to engines with intermittent combustion (two- and four-stroke pistons), but a second class — gas turbines, jet engines, and most rocket engines — uses continuous combustion, and all are classified as internal combustion engines.

quiz_question: Within what scope does a patent give rights to its owner?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a patent is jurisdictional — it only provides rights within the jurisdiction (country or countries) in which it was obtained. An invention patented in the US has no legal protection in Japan unless separately patented there.

quiz_question: What does the Latin root of the word 'inventor' (invenire) literally mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the word 'inventor' comes from the Latin verb invenire, invent-, meaning 'to find.' This etymology frames invention as discovery rather than pure creation — aligning with many inventors who describe finding solutions that were always latent in nature.

quiz_question: What term describes an innovative social behaviour adopted and passed on through culture?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, another meaning of invention is cultural invention — an innovative set of useful social behaviours adopted by people and passed on to others. Language, money, marriage, and democratic voting are all cultural inventions in this sense.

quiz_question: What does the Latin root of 'vehicle' (vehere) mean in English?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'vehicle' derives from Latin vehiculum, itself from vehere meaning 'to carry.' Every form of vehicle — car, ship, airplane, spacecraft — shares this identity: they are all carrying machines.

quiz_question: What technology allows maglev trains to float above their tracks?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, vehicles can use non-contact technologies such as maglev (magnetic levitation) instead of wheels, tracks, rails, or skis. Maglev trains have no physical contact with the ground, reducing friction to near zero and enabling very high speeds.

quiz_question: When a battery is powering a device, which terminal is the source of electrons?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the terminal marked negative is the source of electrons. Negatively charged electrons flow through the circuit from the negative terminal back to the positive terminal, causing a redox reaction that releases the battery's stored energy.

quiz_question: What determines whether a battery is primary or secondary?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, primary (single-use) batteries are discarded after one discharge because electrode materials are irreversibly changed. Secondary (rechargeable) batteries can be restored by applying reverse current. Common examples: alkaline AA cells (primary) and lithium-ion laptop batteries (secondary).

quiz_question: What advantage of electric motors offsets batteries' lower energy density versus gasoline?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, batteries have much lower specific energy than common fuels such as gasoline. In automobiles, this is somewhat offset by the higher efficiency of electric motors in converting electrical energy to mechanical work compared to combustion engines.

quiz_question: What does the 'A' in LASER stand for?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata (official name field), LASER stands for Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation. It became so ubiquitous that most people use it as a normal word without knowing it's an abbreviation.

quiz_question: Where did Theodore Maiman build the world's first laser in 1960?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first laser was built in 1960 by Theodore Maiman at Hughes Research Laboratories, based on theoretical work by Charles Townes and Arthur Schawlow. Wikidata confirms the invention date as May 16, 1960.

quiz_question: What property allows a laser beam to stay narrow over long distances?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a laser differs from other light sources in that it emits coherent light. Spatial coherence allows it to be focused to a tight spot and to stay narrow over great distances (collimation), properties exploited in laser pointers, lidar, and fiber-optic communications.

quiz_question: At what caliber does a fully automatic firearm become an autocannon rather than a machine gun?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, automatic firearms of 20 mm caliber or more are classified as autocannons rather than machine guns. Machine guns are specifically fully automatic and rifled, designed for sustained direct fire — typically mounted on a bipod, tripod, or heavy platform to manage recoil.

quiz_question: What two design features distinguish many machine guns from standard infantry rifles?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, many machine guns use belt feeding and open bolt operation — features not normally found on other infantry firearms. Belt feeding allows sustained fire without reloading magazines; open bolt operation lets the barrel cool between bursts.

quiz_question: What are the two main physical principles that keep aircraft airborne?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, aircraft counter gravity through either static lift (buoyancy, as in hot air balloons and blimps) or the dynamic lift produced by an airfoil shape as air flows over a wing. A few aircraft also use direct downward thrust.

quiz_question: How can an unmanned aerial vehicle fly without a human aboard?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) — commonly called drones — may be remotely controlled by a pilot on the ground or operate fully autonomously via onboard computers without any human intervention.

quiz_question: Under U.S. federal law, what qualifies as an 'aircraft'?
correct_answer: Any device for flight in air
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Title 14 of the U.S. Code of Federal Regulations defines 'aircraft' as any device used or intended to be used for flight in the air — a deliberately broad definition that covers everything from hang gliders to jumbo jets.

quiz_question: How many people worldwide lack access to any toilet?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 419 million people have no toilet access and must openly defecate — a sanitation gap that spreads diseases like cholera and dysentery. This is why the UN included toilet access in Sustainable Development Goal 6.

quiz_question: Which country is globally famous for the most advanced toilet technology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Japan is internationally recognized for pioneering toilet technology, including dual flushing, seat warming, self-cleaning mechanisms, and female urinals — far beyond standard Western designs.

quiz_question: Where is the squat toilet posture more popular than the sitting posture?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sitting toilets (with toilet seats) are the norm in Europe and North America, whereas squat toilets — which require a crouching posture — are more prevalent across Asia.

quiz_question: Who built the first steam engine able to transmit continuous mechanical power?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while Thomas Savery built the first commercially used steam pump and Jerónimo de Ayanz patented a steam-powered water pump in 1606, it was Thomas Newcomen in 1712 who first built an engine that could transmit continuous power to a machine.

quiz_question: What key improvement did James Watt make to the steam engine in 1764?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Watt realized that condensing steam inside the main cylinder wasted huge amounts of energy reheating it. By routing spent steam to a separate condensing vessel, he greatly improved the work obtained per unit of fuel — the key efficiency breakthrough of the Industrial Revolution.

quiz_question: Which country still manufactures large reciprocating piston steam engines as of 2023?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, despite steam engines being largely replaced by electric motors and internal combustion engines in the 20th century, Germany continues to manufacture large reciprocating piston steam engines as of 2023.

quiz_question: Which type of printer builds physical three-dimensional objects rather than flat images?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while most printers output human-readable text or graphics on paper, 3D printers construct physical three-dimensional objects — a radical expansion of the word 'printer' beyond what early inventors imagined.

quiz_question: What output does a barcode printer produce that humans cannot read directly?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while most printer output is human-readable (text or images), barcode printers are a clear example of expanded printer use — their output is designed to be scanned and read by machines, not human eyes.

quiz_question: How many consecutive years did IBM hold the U.S. annual patent record?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, IBM held the U.S. annual patent record from 1993 to 2021 — 29 consecutive years — making it the world's most prolific inventor as measured by granted patents during that span.

quiz_question: Which company invented both the ATM and the hard disk drive?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, IBM's innovation record includes the ATM, DRAM, floppy disk, hard disk drive, magnetic stripe card, relational database, SQL programming language, and Universal Product Code barcode, among many others.

quiz_question: What was IBM's original name when founded in 1911?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, IBM was founded on June 16, 1911, as the Computing-Tabulating-Recording Company (CTR), a holding company of record-keeping and measuring system manufacturers, before being renamed 'International Business Machines' in 1924.

quiz_question: Which probe, besides the Voyagers and Pioneers, is also leaving the Solar System?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, only a handful of interstellar probes — Pioneer 10 and 11, Voyager 1 and 2, and New Horizons — are on trajectories that leave the Solar System. Of all spacecraft ever launched, only these five will travel beyond our Sun's gravitational influence.

quiz_question: What happens to most spacecraft after they complete their mission?
distractors: They dock at a space station
explanation: According to Wikipedia, most spacecraft are not recoverable after their missions. Only a small subset are designed for reentry — further divided into reusable ones like the SpaceX Dragon and Space Shuttle, and expendable ones like Soyuz capsules.

quiz_question: Roughly how many nations have developed independent orbital launch capability?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, only Russia, the U.S., ESA member states, Japan, China, India, Taiwan, Israel, Iran, and North Korea have orbital launch technology, plus private companies like SpaceX and Blue Origin that independently achieved this.

quiz_question: Who invented the temperature scale still officially used in the United States?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit is listed as a discoverer/inventor of the thermometer. His mercury-in-glass thermometer and the Fahrenheit scale — still the official temperature measurement in the U.S. — both bear his name.

quiz_question: Which ancient Greek inventor is among the credited creators of the thermometer?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Hero of Alexandria is listed as a discoverer or inventor of the thermometer. The ancient Greek engineer, known for steam-powered devices like the aeolipile, also devised early air-expansion thermoscopes — crude temperature indicators over 1,900 years ago.

quiz_question: What two elements does every thermometer share?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, all thermometers share two core elements: a temperature sensor (such as a mercury bulb or infrared sensor) in which something physically changes with temperature, and a way to convert that change into a numerical readout.

quiz_question: How many Nobel laureates have been affiliated with MIT as of October 2024?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of October 2024, MIT can claim 105 Nobel laureates, 26 Turing Award winners, and 8 Fields Medalists among its alumni, faculty, and researchers — remarkable for a school with roughly 11,000 students.

quiz_question: What does MIT's Latin motto 'mens et manus' mean in English?
explanation: According to Wikidata, MIT's motto is 'mens et manus' — Latin for 'mind and hand'. It reflects the institution's founding philosophy of fusing theoretical knowledge with practical, hands-on engineering.

quiz_question: Below what family income does MIT waive undergraduate tuition?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, MIT generally does not charge tuition to undergraduates from families with incomes below $200,000 per year. Most graduate students are also funded through research assistantships.

quiz_question: Who is generally credited with inventing the first sewing machine?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the sewing machine is generally considered to have been invented by Englishman Thomas Saint in 1790 during the first Industrial Revolution, to reduce manual sewing work in clothing manufacturing. Wikidata confirms: discoverer or inventor: Thomas Saint; time of discovery: 1790.

quiz_question: How were the first sewing machines powered before electricity?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, early sewing machines were powered either by constantly turning a flywheel handle or by using a foot-operated treadle mechanism. Electrically powered machines were introduced later, greatly increasing speed and ease of use.

quiz_question: How do industrial sewing machines differ from home models?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, home sewing machines are designed for one person to sew individual items using a single stitch type at a time, while industrial sewing machines are larger, faster, and far more varied in size, cost, appearance, and task specialization.

quiz_question: Who patented the first steam-powered water pump in 1606?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1606 Jerónimo de Ayanz y Beaumont patented his invention of the first steam-powered water pump for draining mines — predating the more famous steam pioneers by over a century.

quiz_question: Which tech company invented both the ATM and the hard disk drive?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, IBM's list of foundational technology inventions includes the ATM, DRAM, floppy disk, hard disk drive, magnetic stripe card, relational database, SQL programming language, and UPC barcode — technologies still used by billions of people every day.

quiz_question: How many consecutive years did IBM hold the record for most annual US patents?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, IBM held the record for most annual US patents generated by a business for 29 consecutive years from 1993 to 2021. IBM is also the largest industrial research organization in the world, with 19 research facilities across a dozen countries.

quiz_question: How many Nobel laureates have been affiliated with MIT as of 2024?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of October 2024, MIT claims 105 Nobel laureates, 26 Turing Award winners (the computing equivalent of the Nobel), and 8 Fields Medalists (the equivalent for mathematics) among its alumni, faculty, and researchers.

quiz_question: Below what family income does MIT charge undergraduates no tuition?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, MIT generally does not charge tuition to undergraduates from families with incomes below $200,000, making one of the world's top universities financially accessible to middle-income families. Most graduate students are also funded by research grants.

quiz_question: Who is generally credited with inventing the sewing machine in 1790?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the sewing machine is generally considered to have been invented by Englishman Thomas Saint in 1790. The invention greatly improved efficiency and productivity in the clothing industry, which had previously relied entirely on hand sewing.

quiz_question: How many human-made spacecraft are on interstellar trajectories leaving the Solar System?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, only a handful of interstellar probes — Pioneer 10, Pioneer 11, Voyager 1, Voyager 2, and New Horizons — are on trajectories that will eventually leave the Solar System. Voyager 1 is currently the farthest human-made object from Earth.

quiz_question: Roughly how many people worldwide lack access to any toilet?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 419 million people had no access to a toilet at all, forcing open defecation. This contributes to the spread of waterborne diseases like cholera and dysentery. The UN's Sustainable Development Goal 6 specifically targets universal sanitation access.

quiz_question: What was IBM originally named when it was founded in 1911?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, IBM was founded in 1911 as the Computing-Tabulating-Recording Company (CTR), a holding company of manufacturers of record-keeping and measuring systems. It was not renamed IBM until 1924, at which point it soon became the leading maker of punch-card systems.

quiz_question: What happens to data stored in RAM when you turn off a computer?
distractors: It moves to the hard drive
explanation: According to Wikipedia, RAM is normally associated with volatile memory — stored information is lost if power is removed. This is why unsaved work in a program disappears if the computer crashes: it was held in RAM, not written to permanent storage.

quiz_question: Who invented modern DRAM architecture in 1966?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1966 Dr. Robert Dennard invented modern DRAM architecture, using a single MOS transistor per capacitor. The first commercial DRAM IC chip — the 1K Intel 1103 — was introduced in October 1970, based on his architecture.

quiz_question: In which decade was the QWERTY keyboard layout developed for typewriters?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the QWERTY keyboard layout was developed for typewriters in the 1870s and remains the de facto standard for English-language computer keyboards today. Interestingly, Wikipedia notes the exact origins of this layout still need to be clarified.

quiz_question: In what year were the first commercial typewriters introduced?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first commercial typewriters were introduced in 1874 but did not become common in US offices until after the mid-1880s. The typewriter also served as an early accessibility tool — its use for the blind helped accelerate its development.

quiz_question: Where did the horizontal windmill first appear, in the 9th century?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the horizontal windmill first appeared in Persia during the 9th century, and the vertical windmill appeared in northwestern Europe in the 12th century. Despite the Netherlands being the iconic windmill country today, the technology originated much further east.

quiz_question: Roughly how many windmills remain in the Netherlands today?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there are approximately 1,000 windmills in the Netherlands today. Windmills are regarded as an icon of Dutch culture, historically used to mill grain and pump water in a country largely reclaimed from the sea.

quiz_question: Which country invented fireworks and remains the world's largest fireworks exporter?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fireworks were originally invented in China, which continues to be the largest manufacturer and exporter of fireworks globally. The first skyrockets were also used in warfare before becoming entertainment devices.

quiz_question: Who was the primary hardware designer of the Apple II in 1977?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Steve Wozniak was the primary designer of the Apple II, introduced in 1977 and recognised as one of the first highly successful mass-produced microcomputers. Steve Jobs oversaw the development of its plastic case, while Wozniak handled the core computer engineering.

quiz_question: Which two countries, alongside the US, grant Steve Wozniak citizenship?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Steve Wozniak holds citizenship in the United States, Poland, and Serbia — an unusual triple nationality for one of Silicon Valley's founding engineers. He was born in San Jose, California in 1950.

quiz_question: What is the popular term for the Microsoft Windows plus Intel x86 PC platform combination?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, since the early 1990s the Wintel platform — Microsoft operating systems (first MS-DOS, then Windows) combined with Intel x86 architecture CPUs — has dominated the personal computer market. The term 'PC' now essentially refers to this platform.

quiz_question: Which metal produces the red color in fireworks?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fireworks may be designed to burn with coloured flames and sparks including red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple, and silver. Red colour in fireworks is produced by strontium compounds — a fact from the chemistry of spectroscopy that also has applications in lab analysis.

quiz_question: What device did Steve Wozniak invent through his company CL 9 in 1987?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after permanently leaving Apple in 1985, Wozniak founded CL 9 and created the first programmable universal remote, released in 1987. He then pursued several other business and philanthropic ventures, focusing largely on technology in K-12 schools.

quiz_question: In what year is the personal computer generally considered to have first appeared?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the time of discovery or invention of the personal computer is given as 1957. Institutions in the 1960s had to write their own programs to use computers at all — the idea of a computer for personal use was still decades from mass adoption.

quiz_question: Beyond moving up and down, what unusual direction can some modern elevators travel?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, some modern elevators can even move horizontally — an innovation that challenges the traditional definition of a lift as purely a vertical transport machine. Standard elevators use electric motors driving traction cables and counterweight systems.

quiz_question: What aspect ratio did computer monitors and TVs switch to from 4:3 since 2010?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, since 2010 the typical display aspect ratio of both televisions and computer monitors changed from 4:3 to 16:9. This shift to widescreen fundamentally changed how content is filmed, displayed, and consumed worldwide.

quiz_question: What everyday use of a lens works by concentrating sunlight via refraction?
explanation: According to Wikidata, one documented use of a lens is firelighting — focusing sunlight to a point hot enough to ignite tinder. Wikipedia explains that a lens focuses light to form an image by refraction, unlike a prism which refracts light without focusing.

quiz_question: What software translates high-level code into machine-executable instructions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a compiler is software that translates computer code written in one programming language (the source language) into another language (the target language), typically from a high-level language into low-level machine code to create an executable program.

quiz_question: How many simple machines were identified by Renaissance scientists, including the lever?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the lever is one of the six simple machines identified by Renaissance scientists. A lever amplifies an input force to provide a greater output force — providing mechanical advantage equal to the ratio of output force to input force — by trading force against movement.

quiz_question: In what year was the electric motor invented?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the electric motor was invented in 1834. Wikipedia notes that the largest electric motors are used for marine propulsion, pipeline compression, and pumped-storage applications, with output exceeding 100 megawatts — while the smallest are found in watches.

quiz_question: Who created the first GMO — a bacterium — in 1973?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first GMO was a bacterium generated by Herbert Boyer and Stanley Cohen in 1973. The first recombinant DNA molecule had been designed by Paul Berg in 1972. Rudolf Jaenisch created the first GM animal (a mouse) in 1974.

quiz_question: What was the first genetically modified food sold commercially, released in 1994?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, genetically modified food has been sold since 1994 with the release of the Flavr Savr tomato — engineered to have a longer shelf life. Most current GM crops are modified to increase resistance to insects and herbicides rather than shelf life.

quiz_question: How did Rudolf Diesel, inventor of the diesel engine, die in 1913?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Rudolf Diesel's cause of death was drowning, and his place of death was the English Channel. His body was discovered in the sea in 1913 — whether by accident, suicide, or foul play has never been definitively established.

quiz_question: Which inventor has both an engine type AND a fuel type named after him?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Rudolf Diesel (1858–1913) was a German inventor and mechanical engineer best known for inventing the diesel engine, which burns diesel fuel — and both are named after him. Very few inventors in history have had both a machine type and a fuel type bear their name.

quiz_question: English is the world's most spoken language overall, but what rank does it hold for native speakers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, English is the most spoken language in the world primarily due to the global influence of the British Empire and the United States — but it is only the third most spoken native language, after Mandarin Chinese and Spanish.

quiz_question: What percentage of Modern English words come from French dialects?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, French dialects are the source of approximately 28% of Modern English words, and Latin is the source of an additional 28%. Despite this Romance-language vocabulary dominance, English's grammar and phonology remain Germanic, as does most basic everyday vocabulary.

quiz_question: What device is mechanically identical to an electric motor but converts motion into electricity?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, an electric generator is mechanically identical to an electric motor but operates in reverse — converting mechanical energy into electrical energy. In regenerative braking, motors in electric vehicles run as generators to recover otherwise lost heat energy.

quiz_question: What aspect ratio replaced the square-ish 4:3 format on most screens since 2010?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, since 2010 the typical display aspect ratio of both televisions and computer monitors changed from 4:3 to 16:9. Before the mid-2000s, most monitors used cathode ray tubes (CRTs) as their display technology.

quiz_question: How many simple machines did Renaissance scientists identify, of which the lever is one?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Renaissance scientists identified six simple machines: the lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, and screw. A lever amplifies force by trading off force against movement via a fulcrum.

quiz_question: Which country has the most native Spanish speakers in the world?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the country with the largest population of native Spanish speakers is Mexico. Spanish has 519 million native speakers globally, making it the world's second-most spoken native language after Mandarin Chinese.

quiz_question: How many countries have Spanish as an official language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Spanish is the official language of 20 countries, as well as one of the six official languages of the United Nations. It is the world's second-most spoken native language after Mandarin Chinese.

quiz_question: Which language has the most native speakers within the European Union?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, German is the most spoken native language within the European Union. It has nearly 80 million native speakers and over 130 million total speakers as of 2024. It is also the second-most commonly used language in science.

quiz_question: Which major European language capitalises all nouns, not just proper nouns?
explanation: According to Wikidata, German uses capitalisation for all nouns — a feature that distinguishes it from virtually all other major European languages, which only capitalise proper nouns. This applies to common nouns like 'table' (Tisch) and 'dog' (Hund).

quiz_question: Russian is one of only two official languages aboard which iconic space structure?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Russian is one of two official languages aboard the International Space Station, the other being English. Russian is also one of the six official languages of the United Nations and the most geographically widespread language of Eurasia.

quiz_question: Arabic is the liturgical language of which world religion, connecting over 2 billion people?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Arabic is the liturgical language of Islam and is used by more than 2 billion Muslims worldwide for prayer. It is also the fifth-most spoken language in the world with around 380 million speakers.

quiz_question: Which ancient trade language gave European languages words like algebra, alcohol, and coffee?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, many European languages borrowed words from Arabic — especially Spanish and to a lesser extent Portuguese, Catalan, and others — owing to Arabic cultural and linguistic presence in Southern Iberia during the Al-Andalus era.

quiz_question: How many different writing systems does Japanese use simultaneously in everyday text?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Japanese writing system combines kanji (Chinese characters), hiragana, and katakana — two syllabaries derived by the Japanese from more complex Chinese characters. Latin script (rōmaji) is also used in limited contexts.

quiz_question: How many countries have French as an official language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, French is an official language in 26 countries and spoken in about 50 countries total, with most being members of the Organisation internationale de la Francophonie. It has approximately 310 million speakers worldwide.

quiz_question: What is the fifth-most spoken native language in the world, with 267 million speakers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Portuguese has approximately 267 million speakers and is listed as the fifth-most spoken native language. It is spoken chiefly in Portugal, Brazil, and several African countries.

quiz_question: On what notable date was YouTube founded in 2005?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata (inception: 2005-02-14), YouTube was founded on February 14, 2005 — Valentine's Day — by Chad Hurley, Jawed Karim, and Steve Chen, all former PayPal employees. Google purchased it on November 13, 2006 for $1.65 billion.

quiz_question: How many hours of video are uploaded to YouTube every minute?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of May 2019, videos were being uploaded to YouTube at a rate of more than 500 hours of content per minute. That means more video arrives in one minute than a human could watch in three weeks nonstop.

quiz_question: How much did YouTube earn in advertising revenue in 2023?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2023 YouTube's advertising revenue totalled $31.7 billion, a 2% increase from $31.1 billion in 2022. From Q4 2023 to Q3 2024, combined advertising and subscription revenue exceeded $50 billion.

quiz_question: What does the name Esperanto translate to in English?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Esperanto was created by L. L. Zamenhof in 1887, published under the pseudonym Doktoro Esperanto. The word Esperanto translates to English as "one who hopes." It was designed as a universal second language for international communication.

quiz_question: What makes Esperanto unique among all constructed languages?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Esperanto is the most successful constructed international auxiliary language, and the only such language with a sizeable population of native speakers (called denaskuloj in Esperanto), estimated at around 2,000. Total speakers are estimated at around 100,000.

quiz_question: How many official languages does the International Space Station use?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Russian is one of two official languages aboard the International Space Station (the other being English), as well as one of the six official languages of the United Nations. It is also the most spoken native language in all of Europe.

quiz_question: Which language has the longest documented written history of any Indo-European language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Greek is an Indo-European language with at least 3,400 years of written records — the longest documented history of any Indo-European language. Its writing system has been in continuous use for approximately 2,800 years.

quiz_question: Which language is the only successful large-scale revival of a dead spoken language in history?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hebrew is the only successful large-scale example of linguistic revival. It ceased to be a regular spoken language between 200 and 400 CE, then was revived in the 19th century under Zionism, led by Eliezer Ben-Yehuda.

quiz_question: In what year did Turkey switch from the Arabic-based Ottoman script to the Latin alphabet?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1928, as one of Ataturk's reforms in the early Republic of Turkey, the Perso-Arabic script-based Ottoman Turkish alphabet was replaced with the Latin script-based Turkish alphabet. The change happened rapidly, making the entire existing literate population functionally illiterate overnight in their old writing system.

quiz_question: Why do musicians worldwide use Italian words like piano, forte, and allegro?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Italian has significant use in musical terminology and opera, with numerous Italian words referring to music that have become international terms taken into various languages worldwide including English. This stems from Italy's dominant role in developing classical music and opera from the 16th century.

quiz_question: How many distinct writing systems can appear together in a single Japanese sentence?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, the Japanese writing system combines Chinese characters (kanji), hiragana, katakana, and sometimes Latin script (romaji). A single Japanese sentence might use all four systems — something no other major language does routinely.

quiz_question: Which Indo-European language has the longest documented written history — over 3,400 years?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Greek language has the longest documented history of any Indo-European language, spanning at least 3,400 years of written records — from Linear B tablets to Modern Greek today.

quiz_question: Which two ancient languages remain the main source of modern scientific terminology worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Greek and Latin are highly influential on the English language and remain the predominant sources of international scientific vocabulary. When scientists name a new virus, disease, or organism, they still reach for Greek and Latin roots.

quiz_question: Which is the only language ever successfully revived as a native spoken tongue after centuries of disuse?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hebrew ceased to be a regular spoken language around 200-400 CE but was revived in the 19th century, led by Eliezer Ben-Yehuda. It is described as the only successful large-scale example of linguistic revival and became the official language of Israel.

quiz_question: Which is the only surviving language from the ancient Canaanite language family?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hebrew is the only Canaanite language still spoken today. Related Canaanite languages such as Phoenician, Moabite, and Ammonite are all extinct. Hebrew is also one of only two Northwest Semitic languages with contemporary speakers (the other being Aramaic).

quiz_question: What percentage of the global population speaks an Indo-European language as a first language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, over 3.4 billion people (42% of the global population) speak an Indo-European language as a first language — by far the most of any language family. This family includes English, Spanish, Hindi, Russian, Portuguese, French, and hundreds more.

quiz_question: Which major language uses a single pronoun for he, she, and it — with no grammatical gender?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Turkish has no noun classes or grammatical gender. The single third-person pronoun "o" covers he, she, and it — in sharp contrast to European languages like French, German, and Spanish where nearly every noun has an assigned gender.

quiz_question: Which Romance language ranks fifth most spoken natively in the world, with around 267 million speakers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, with approximately 267 million speakers, Portuguese is listed as the fifth most spoken native language. It is spoken chiefly in Portugal, Brazil, and several African and Asian countries — one of the most geographically widespread languages on Earth.

quiz_question: What surprising directional capability do some advanced modern elevators now have?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, some elevators can even move horizontally. Modern smart elevators also enable multi-elevator coordination, enhancing the riding experience while reducing costs. This opens entirely new possibilities for skyscraper and interconnected building design.

quiz_question: What key ability does a lens have that a prism does NOT?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a lens can focus light to form an image, unlike a prism, which refracts light without focusing. This focusing ability is what makes lenses essential for cameras, telescopes, microscopes, and corrective eyeglasses.

quiz_question: Which language is the primary source of international musical and opera terminology worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Italian has significant use in musical terminology and opera, with numerous Italian words referring to music that have become international terms taken into various languages worldwide — including piano, forte, allegro, soprano, and tempo.

quiz_question: Which language served as the official court language of both the Mughal and Ottoman Empires, despite not being native to either?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Persian was used officially as a language of bureaucracy by non-native speakers — the Ottomans in Anatolia, the Mughals in South Asia, and the Pashtuns in Afghanistan. It was considered a prestige language across West, Central, and South Asia for centuries.

quiz_question: What two resources do modern washing machines primarily consume to clean clothes?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, modern washing machines use electric power to automatically clean clothes, with users adding laundry detergent to the wash water. They replace the laborious process of hand-washing that was universal before electrification.

quiz_question: Which language served as the court language of the Mughal Empire, despite not being native to South Asia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Persian was used officially as a language of bureaucracy even by non-native speakers such as the Ottomans in Anatolia and the Mughals in South Asia. It was the first language to break through Arabic's monopoly on writing in the Muslim world.

quiz_question: What percentage of the global population speaks a variety of Chinese as a first language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, approximately 1.39 billion people, or 17% of the global population, speak one of the varieties of Chinese as their first language. Mandarin alone accounts for about 66% of Chinese speakers, with around 800 million speakers.

quiz_question: In which century was the Korean Hangul writing system deliberately designed and created?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Korean script (Hangul) is an alphabet system developed during the 15th century. It uses 24 basic letters (jamo) and 27 complex letters. Unlike most writing systems that evolved organically, Hangul was intentionally designed — by King Sejong the Great — for the Korean language.

quiz_question: What makes the Rigveda's oral transmission over millennia extraordinarily unusual?
distractors: It was written in invisible ink
It was written on clay tablets only
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Rigveda was orally transmitted by methods of exceptional complexity, rigour, and fidelity, preserved as a single text without variant readings. This makes its preserved archaic syntax and morphology vital for reconstructing Proto-Indo-European.

quiz_question: What percentage of the global population speaks an Indo-European language as their first language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, over 3.4 billion people (42% of the global population) speak an Indo-European language as a first language — by far the most of any language family. English, Spanish, Hindi, Portuguese, and Russian are all members of this family.

quiz_question: Which language replaced Dutch as an official language of South Africa in 1925?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Dutch was one of the official languages of South Africa until 1925, when it was replaced by Afrikaans — a separate but partially mutually intelligible daughter language that evolved from Cape Dutch dialects and is spoken by at least 16 million people.

quiz_question: Hindi is the fourth-most-spoken first language in the world — which three languages rank above it?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hindi is the fourth-most-spoken first language in the world, after Mandarin, Spanish, and English. With Urdu included (due to mutual intelligibility), it ranks third. Wikidata records over 274 million native Hindi speakers.

quiz_question: What share of the global population speaks Chinese as a first language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, approximately 1.39 billion people, or 17% of the global population, speak one of the Chinese varieties as their first language. Mandarin alone accounts for about 66% of all Chinese speakers, with around 800 million speakers.

quiz_question: How do most linguists classify the varieties of Chinese despite official Chinese government policy?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while the Chinese government defines all spoken Chinese varieties as merely diverse dialects of a single language, the frequent lack of mutual intelligibility has led linguists to consider them separate languages within a language family. Classifications posit 7 to 13 main regional groups.

quiz_question: What percentage of Swahili vocabulary comes from Arabic loanwords?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, around 40% of Swahili vocabulary consists of Arabic loanwords, including the name 'Swahili' itself (from Arabic meaning 'of the coasts'). This borrowing dates from the era of Arab traders along Africa's east coast.

quiz_question: Roughly how many total speakers does Swahili have, including second-language users?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, estimates of the number of Swahili speakers generally range from 150 million to 200 million, including both native and second-language speakers. The UN declared 7 July as Swahili Language Day in 2022.

quiz_question: How many hymns does the Rigveda, the oldest Sanskrit text, contain?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Rigveda contains 1,028 hymns composed between 1500 and 1200 BCE by Indo-Aryan tribes. It was orally transmitted by memorization of exceptional complexity and fidelity, as a single text without variant readings.

quiz_question: When was the Korean Hangul alphabet invented?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Korean is written in the Korean script (Hangul), an alphabet system developed during the 15th century for that purpose, although it did not become the primary script until the mid-20th century. Hanja (Chinese characters) and mixed script were the primary script until then.

quiz_question: How many grammatical cases does Hungarian have?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Hungarian has an extensive case system including nominative, accusative, dative, genitive, and many locative and other cases. With 14 million speakers, it is the most widely spoken Uralic language.

quiz_question: Which ancient alphabet is the ultimate ancestor of Arabic, Greek, Latin, Hebrew, and Cyrillic?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first fully phonemic script was the Proto-Sinaitic script, which was later modified to create the Phoenician alphabet. The Phoenician system is considered the first true alphabet and is the ultimate ancestor of Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew, Latin, and possibly Brahmic scripts.
